## Argv0Resolution interface

The result of following a [CommandSite](./sh-ast-analyze.commandsite.md)<!-- -->'s argv0 through zero or more transparent wrappers to the effective command actually invoked. Facts only â€” no safety verdict; see [resolveArgv0()](./sh-ast-analyze.resolveargv0.md)<!-- -->'s doc comment.

**Signature:**

```typescript
export interface Argv0Resolution 
```

number


</td><td>

The number of `VAR=val` shell-assignment prefixes on the `CallExpr` itself (`CommandSite.node.assigns`<!-- -->, e.g. the `FOO=bar` in `FOO=bar rm x`<!-- -->) that were skipped to reach argv0. This is a distinct mechanism from a wrapper's own `VAR=val` \*operands\* (e.g. `env`<!-- -->'s or `sudo`<!-- -->'s, see [WrapperSpec.skipAssignmentOperands](./sh-ast-analyze.wrapperspec.skipassignmentoperands.md)<!-- -->) â€” always exactly `CommandSite.node.assigns`<!-- -->'s length, independent of the wrapper chain found afterward.

</td><td>

Every word resolved along the way, outermost wrapper first, ending with [Argv0Resolution.effective](./sh-ast-analyze.argv0resolution.effective.md) â€” e.g. for `nohup env A=1 command rm x` this is the four resolutions for `nohup`<!-- -->, `env`<!-- -->, `command`<!-- -->, `rm`<!-- -->, in that order. Always has at least one element.


</td></tr>

</td><td>

[Argv0ChainWord](./sh-ast-analyze.argv0chainword.md)


</td><td>

The last element of [Argv0Resolution.chain](./sh-ast-analyze.argv0resolution.chain.md) â€” the effective command, or, if resolution hit a statically-unknowable word anywhere along the chain (an expansion, a glob, an unrecognized flag, an embedded-command operand, â€¦), that unknowable result itself. \*\*A `static: false` word is never guessed through\*\*: the moment one is reached (whether at argv0, several wrappers deep, or synthesized by `resolveArgv0` itself for an unrecognized flag), it becomes `effective` and the chain stops â€” this is the security-relevant guarantee that makes `sudo -u x "$prog"` report an unknowable effective command rather than silently treating `sudo` itself as the answer, and makes `sudo -D /tmp rm x` (an unrecognized `sudo` flag) report `'unknown-flag'` rather than misreporting `rm` â€” or, worse, `/tmp` â€” as the effective command.


</td></tr>
